turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: We will hear argument this morning in Case 24-1238, Montgomery versus Caribe Transport. Mr. Clement.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: When Congress ended federal economic regulation of the transportation industry, it did not want states to fill the deregulatory gap with economic regulation of their own. It thus enacted a preemption clause modeled on the Airline Deregulation Act that preempts state laws related to prices, routes, or services of motor carriers, of brokers, with respect to the transportation of property. But, while Congress favored economic deregulation, the last thing it wanted was safety deregulation. To that end, it preserved federal safety regulatory authority, and it accepted state fed -- state reg -- safety regulatory authority with respect to motor vehicles. My friends concede that state tort law is a classic exercise of regulatory authority and that a principal aim of tort law is safety. Thus, the question in this case boils down to whether the negligent hiring claim here is with respect to motor vehicles. Of course, it is. The whole point of the tort is to keep dangerous motor vehicles off of the road. Indeed, my friends concede as much when the tort is directed at a carrier. But that essentially gives away the game because it's the same tort whether it's directed at a carrier or a broker, and it is no less with respect to motor vehicles when it's directed against a broker who may well know that the vehicles are poorly maintained or the carrier is underinsured or not registered with the federal government at all. The -- nonetheless, my friends would immunize the brokers in all those situations. That cannot be squared with the plain text or with common sense. Indeed, my friends have to insert words into the statute like "direct link" or "direct connection" that are absent from the statutory text. This Court should interpret the statute as written and not let the brokers off scot-free. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Mr. Clement, the -- what did the landscape look like before deregulation? And by that, I mean who was -- was responsible for the safety regulations? Was it the ICC or some other federal agency, or was it the state?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So it was both the federal Transportation Department or subparts of the federal Transportation Department at the federal level and the states at the -- at the state level. So the old ICC regulated rates, prices, and routes, but it did not regulate safety. And that's really the regime, I think, Congress --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Well, the -- the -- the -- I'm more interested in the safety aspect because I think much of what we're talking about will depend on what it means to regulate the safety of motor vehicles, including the operation. So I'm interested if you would detail the safety regime before the ICC was deregulated.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So, before the ICC was deregulated --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Was eliminated. I'm sorry.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Yeah, eliminated. Right. You did have some federal safety regulation from the federal government but -- but not through the ICC, through the Transportation Department. But you also had -- and there was no preemption of it. You also had state safety regulation. And they could take many forms. They could be positive law, regulations that go to safety, everything from speed limits to other provisions, weight limits, maintenance requirements. But you also had these state negligent hiring torts that were applied under the old regime both against carriers and against brokers. And we cite the L.B. Foster case, for example, from the Ninth Circuit in 1969 in our brief, and that's a state negligent hiring claim that was brought against both brokers and against carriers, and the Ninth Circuit upheld it. And, of course, there would have been no basis before 1995 to even argue that that claim against a broker was preempted and no argument before 1994 that that argument -- that that claim was preempted against a carrier.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: One oddity I think that you have to deal with is that your theory would mean that claims against brokers would be -- would be preempted for intrastate transportation but not for interstate transportation, which seems a bit backwards in terms of preemption, and that's something, if you think the question here is how broadly to read "with respect to motor vehicles" and you're thinking about context and common sense, which you mentioned, that seems to be in tension with that. You want to respond to that?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I would love to because I think there is a counter-intuitiveness to the fact that Congress seems to have provided a broader preemption provision for intrastate routes, prices, and services for brokers and freight forwarders and a, you know, smaller preemptive scope for interstate. Now that anomaly is going to exist no matter how you decide this case because (b) is broader not just because it doesn't have a safety exception but also because it doesn't have the phrase "with respect to the transportation of property." So, you know, it -- and -- and so, like, a case like Dan's City, you know, would -- where this Court said that "with respect to the transportation of property" is what meant there was no preemption, there would be preemption for interstate. Now I'm not here to tell you that makes any sense, but what I am here to tell you is that Congress in 1995 made a very deliberate decision to treat brokers and freight forwarders differently for interstate and for intrastate. I mean, it would have been the easiest thing in the world in 1995, because, remember, brokers are added to both a year after --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right. Yeah.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- the principal statute. It would have been the easiest thing in the world to create, like, a subsection that was about brokers and freight forwarders for interstate and intrastate. But Congress didn't do that. They consciously had a separate sub (b) clause for intrastate, and then they consciously dropped the brokers and the freight forwarders into (c), which already had the safety exception.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Just going back to the real world, though, you know, a -- a broker in Los Angeles who arranges a trip to San Francisco, no -- no tort suits available. But, if it's to -- to Reno, a tort suit is available under your theory, right?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Under our theory of the safety exception. Now, you know --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- you might be able --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: On the safety exception --

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- which is the key. And I guess --

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: But -- but --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- I'm still trying to figure out how that makes some sense, particularly when -- let's just add to it the other kind of issue that -- that bothers me a little bit here, is the fact that Congress required financial security requirements for the truckers but not for the brokers and -- with respect to accidents, and that seems another thing that you can -- you can respond to.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Sure. But let me respond to them in turn. So starting just to -- to reiterate what I said with (b), I mean, look, I'm -- I'm not here to tell you that that's not a little bit anomalous, but it would be more anomalous to take two statutes, one of which has a safety exception and one of which doesn't, and interpret them to mean the same thing. And there's no question that (b) is not the applicable provision here, (c) is the applicable provision. And as I said, no matter what you do with the safety exception, you're going to still have an intrastate provision that is -- has broader preemptive force than the interstate provision because (b) doesn't have that with respect to transportation across --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What about the financial security requirement?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: If we're trying to figure out what Congress was doing, which I think you've talked about here in '94 and '95, they say that the truckers have -- the trucking companies have to have financial security so they can pay judgments with respect to accidents, but they don't do that on brokers. And when you combine the two things we're talking about, that does -- I mean, that's in tension with your position. But what's your best response to that?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, I think I have several pretty good responses to that, I mean, one of which is it's even more complicated than that because freight forwarders were also supposed to have some insurance against injury risk. So -- so you can't make some idea that everybody in the sub (b) is different from everybody in the sub (c) if you're following me on that. But more to the point --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I am -- I am. But, if Congress envisioned tort suits against brokers, why not require brokers to have the insurance to pay the judgments?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Because I -- I think Congress rationally said we want to make sure that they have a financial security requirement that is responsive to the principal financial risk that they face. And it's different with respect to carriers than with respect to brokers. The principal financial security risk that brokers have is default, but you can't read too much into the insurance requirements because nobody thinks that carriers can't default. In fact, carriers are defaulting all the time --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: All -- all the time.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- and going bankrupt --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- all the time. And so you can't draw too strong an inference. And what I would also say is there's also sort of an asymmetry in the -- in the inference that you would draw because, you know, some lower courts, for example, have focused on that insurance requirement by finding that safety torts are not preempted in the Airline Deregulatory Act context. But that sort of makes sense because it would be very weird for Congress to force a carrier to insure against a risk that is actually zero because the risk is preempted. So, when -- when Congress says you must insure against something, it's actually a pretty positive sign that there's not preemption. But I don't think it works the other way around. There's all sorts of contexts where you say things are not preempted and Congress hasn't even thought to make somebody insure against it. So I just don't think, you know -- like, you know, I -- I -- like, I don't think there is a presumption against preemption in the express preemption context, but, boy, you need something more than an insurance requirement to ignore the plain text of a clause that is designed to deal with the issue of preemption straight up.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Can I take you back, Mr. Clement, to Justice Kavanaugh's first set of questions, the interstate/intrastate, and you said you're -- you're not here to tell us that it makes any sense. Do -- do -- do you have a theory whether or not it makes any sense about how it came to be? I mean, why?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I think that, you know, it was a different -- it was a different Congress a year later. Maybe the interest groups lobbied a little different a year later. Maybe the same people weren't paying attention a year later. And, again, though, I -- I think the critical thing, though, is the one thing we can't ignore is that Congress made the deliberate judgment to treat interstate and intrastate differently. Now, if -- if you really think they should be the same, then maybe that's a reason to not read the preemption clause to reach safety-related torts altogether, as -- as lower courts have read the ADA. I mean, if the anomaly's really bothering you, that's the one way you can make the anomaly go away. Or, actually, suppose, you know, like, the text of (b) is a little bit different and it's so focused on intrastate rates, intrastate routes, in -- like, intrastate services that you could say that really, unless you're talking specifically about intrastate services, the preemption clause doesn't apply. But, you know, maybe it's best to leave all of that for a case that actually implicates (b) because this is a (c) case, and Congress clearly said, with respect to interstate, at the -- at the moment it's creating this new exception for intrastate, brokers and freight forwarders, at the same moment, it says, you know what we're going to do, we're going to not treat brokers and freight forwarders the same way for interstate and intrastate and when it comes to interstate, we're going to drop them into a -- a preemption provision that has a safety exception. I think that's what you have to sort of try to interpret here. And then, if you get to the safety exception, I think the question of whether these negligent hiring torts against a broker are with respect to motor vehicles is quite straightforward. And I think, if you understand the nature of the 411 Restatement tort, the whole reason that there is a negligent hiring tort in certain circumstances but not others is because you're hiring somebody to do something that has the possibility of harm, bodily harm, to third people -- third persons, rather. And what creates the threat of bodily harm to third persons in this context? It's the 80,000-pound motor vehicle that you're putting somebody behind the wheel of or you're entrusting the -- the cargo to. And so -- and that's true whether it's a -- a tort against the -- the -- the broker or against the carrier. And so, if I hire somebody to just, like, paint my house and they do a lousy job, like, shame on me, but there's no negligent hiring of a contractor tort in that context because just painting the house isn't going to pose a risk of bodily harm to third parties. So what really triggers this whole tort is a risk of bodily harm to third parties, and the only thing in this mix that poses that risk of bodily harm to third parties is the motor vehicle. So, if you follow what tort we're talking about here, it really does make the connection to motor vehicles quite direct.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Mr. Clement, could another way of thinking about this really be focusing on (c), as you've encouraged us to do, and how it operates, what it's doing? It seems to me that one could conceive of this as (c)(1), Congress coming in and making a change to the status quo. This is the deregulation point that you -- that you said. And they say in that section that this deregulation is occurring with respect to the economic aspects of the system. And then the safety clause, this part that we're all focused on now, is really just clarifying to some extent that what they have done with respect to preemption is leaving the status quo regarding safety. And so Justice Thomas's point was -- was a good one; in other words, what was the status quo? States were regulating with respect to safety. So was the federal government to some extent. And if you think of it from that kind of bird's eye view, I think it seems pretty odd for Respondents to suggest that Congress was really focused on who would be able to bring a claim or how. It's sort of -- that's all up to how the state was regulating. What is their tort? What is their system? Congress was just, it seems to me in this statute, saying we're not touching any of that with respect to the preemption that we are now establishing.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So I wouldn't resist one word of that. I would say that's exactly how the lower courts have approached this in the context of the ADA, where you don't have a safety exception, so your eyes don't immediately go to the safety exception. But still, courts have realized that long before -- like in the bad old days of federal reg -- economic regulation, you still had state tort suits for personal injury and the rest. And so, when Congress in the ADA says, all right, we want to get rid of the federal economic regulation and we don't want the states replacing it with new economic regulation of their own, but they had no interest whatsoever in getting rid of all those tort actions, and, as I say, as we say in the briefs, but I think it's quite true, actually, from Congress's perspective, if you want economic deregulation, the last thing you want is a huge uptick in accidents and lots of, like, safety mishaps.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, can I just interrupt there just on the rhetoric. It's not getting rid of safety tort suits. The tort suits against the trucking companies, the carriers, are still preserved. Everyone agrees on that.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Sure. But the problem is, once you agree to that, there's really no coherent reason to stop there. Like, you're looking at the state tort. And the state tort is not a --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I was just responding to your point about safety regulation being gone. No, safety regulation through tort suits still exists, everyone agrees, against the primary people who are going to be sued, as I think you've acknowledged, which are the -- the truckers, the carriers.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Yeah. And I didn't mean to get carried away with my rhetoric. My -- my point was just like, if -- if you're a rational deregulator, but all -- you know, you care about, you -- you -- Chicago school, you want economic freedom, the last thing you want at that same moment is for there to be new safety problems because that --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And -- and my -- my point was -- was why would we think that given the background that you're talking about, that Congress would be focused on preserving only the tort claims with respect to the truckers, as opposed to the brokers, as opposed to anybody else? In other words, why -- Congress's attention, it would seem to me, would not be at the level of who is actually going to ultimately be sued, you know, under tort in these different states or whatnot. The point of its regulation was just to say we are concerned with economic deregulation. Those who are regulating safety, carry on. And under whatever state's law, if they have a particular tort, if they had it before, if it's operating, then Congress wasn't bothering that. If it applied to brokers as well as truckers, Congress wasn't bothering that. It just seems odd to me to think that we would read this statute and all that it's doing to have Congress caring about the particular defendants who are going to be sued at the end of the day.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Again, I couldn't agree more, and I think, if you look at that L.B. Foster case, 1969, Ninth Circuit case, I mean, that's a case where the plaintiff sued the shipper, the broker, and the carrier, and under California law, they were all liable. Now some other state might make a judgment that we want to stop liability at the broker and not have it go to the -- the shipper. Or another state might say we want to stop liability at the -- at the carrier. Maybe a state -- I think it would be crazy, but maybe a state wants to stop it at the driver. All of those are judgments that the states get to make. And I don't think, as you suggested --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So, even if a shipper uses a broker who then hires a motor carrier, you think the shipper is included in this?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I -- I -- I do. I think, in a -- in a sense, that's a much more straightforward case. As long as the state tort reaches them, then the shippers aren't covered by the preemption clause. And I actually -- so I -- I don't think the shippers have much of an argument. You know, you got to -- you got to start with the argument that it's preempted and then resist the safety exception. So the shippers can't get off first base -- get to first base because they're not covered by the preemption clause. And one of the anomalies that the other side's position creates, I mean, you know, they -- they like to talk about the brokers as just being middlemen or middle persons. But they're middlemen between shippers and carriers, who both, at least in some states, are liable for this negligent hiring tort. So it's weird to have the middleman between two people that are potentially liable immune, and if you allow that to happen, it creates a huge artificial incentive for shippers to use brokers because that's a way they can try to download or --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What --

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- and the other thing they can do is --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: What if --

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- they can actually reinvent themselves as -- as -- as brokers --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: As far as --

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- like --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: No, I'm sorry.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Yeah. As -- as far as federal law is concerned, how far does "with respect to motor vehicles" extend? I mean, this is a classic question of causation. Does it extend to anything that could contribute to an accident?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So I'm not sure that it, you know, sort of goes, like, to, you know, the butterfly effect and all of that kind of thing. What this Court has said in lots of contexts is it doesn't have to be, like, super-direct. There's no direct link requirement. But, at the same time, things that are attenuated, peripheral, and remote, I think is the phrase this Court has used on a couple of occasions, are not covered. Now, here, I think it's a pretty easy way to say that this is not wildly indirect for purposes of --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Yeah, no, I -- I see that. But do you think there is some limitation in the phrase "with respect to"?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Yeah. Absolutely. But it's -- but -- but I would say, like, if -- if I were, like, sitting on Congress's shoulder and saying, all right, we want direct connections only, I would say don't use the "with respect to" language.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: And the limitation is proximate cause under state law? Is that the limitation?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, that's one practical limitation in this context because you can't bring a tort against a broker under state law unless you satisfy the proximate cause requirement.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Yeah. Well, I --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: -- understand this is pretty direct, but if something has to be -- some kind of directness requirement or a -- a minimum link requirement has to be read into (c)(2), then I come back to the question that Justice Kavanaugh led up with, which is the real anomaly, which is very hard to accept, that there's preemption for intrastate activity but not for interstate activity, and I came to the argument hoping you were going to give us some brilliant way of reconciling these two provisions other than just live with it, but I guess there's no -- there is no such theory.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: If there is, it has escaped me. But, as I say, I do think it's telling that you're going to have a broader intrastate -- intrastate preemption clause no matter how you decide this case. The other thing is I would say I don't think -- I mean, that would really be letting the tail wag the dog. You know, I've looked through all these cases. I think there are two intrastate cases that I've been able to find. Maybe my friends have found more. But there are, you know, dozens and dozens of interstate cases. And the intrastate cases I've found have actually said there's no preemption on sort of the ADA, that's not what this is all about. So I think it would be a mistake to let the absence of the safety exception in (b) be the tail that wags the dog.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What do you -- what do you do with the fact that the brokers -- I think the amicus brief of the Transportation Intermediaries Association, particularly pages 20 to 24 -- really go in detail of, if tort liability extends to them, how are they going to assess and evaluate the safety of drivers in a particular trucking company? How are they going to figure out do they have an alcohol/drug issue? How are they going to figure out English proficiency, which, of course, is a critical issue at the current moment that's being discussed? How are they going to do this is the real concern that's raised in that brief and by Respondents that I think, you know, is something you need to respond to.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Sure. I'll try to respond to it, and I think it may be much more straightforward than you think in many cases. I mean, ultimately, like, the legal answer is it's the "know or should have known" standard. But, in --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But, usually, when you say that -- sorry to interrupt -- you know, there's some way for the tortfeasor to -- to protect themselves, and -- and what they're saying here is, in the real world of how brokers operate, it's very difficult.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So I think it's going to be pretty easy for them to protect themselves by hiring quality carriers. And --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And that's going to favor the -- so this whole thing's going to really favor the large established carriers over the smaller carriers because they're going to default to that to protect themselves, I think, which is fine, but I just want the implications of your position to be understood.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Look, you can be a safe small carrier. And here's the thing, and this is where --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: They tend -- they tend not to be as safe as the larger ones, correct?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, some of them tend to be very unsafe, and some of them tend to be --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- these chameleon carriers where they -- their safety record is terrible and then they show back up. And the situations where there should be liability is when the broker knows, like, or should know that it's a chameleon carrier. If they've just been dealing with this carrier as, you know, Joe's Trucking run by Joe Smith, and then all of a sudden Joe Smith shows up and this time it's Smith Trucking, like, the carrier is going to know that -- the broker's going to know that. And here's one of the things that sort of helped me understand all of this, which is you have a number of situations where there is a captive carrier, where that carrier does only loads for C.H. Robinson. And so, in that circumstance, C.H. Robinson is going to know every bit as much as the carrier is going to know in those circumstances. Now there are going to be other situations, like, say, they can protect themselves, but they can also protect themselves by asking some questions and having some screens. I mean, this is a case where C.H. Robinson's own policy says that they don't hire carriers that have conditional safety ratings, but they did it here anyways. So, you know -- so you have cases like that where, yeah, you can worry a little bit about how they're going to protect themselves, but they already take some efforts. And when they blow their own sort of standards or, like --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, they were relying on the federal government standards because the federal government regulates the trucking companies and -- and kind of certifies them.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: In a -- in a very --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And that's probably not good enough, is what you're saying, which I -- I don't -- and I'm not taking issue with that, but -- but that's what they've been relying on in the past, right?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: But the federal government regulates in a very minimal way, and the federal statute specifically says it's a minimum, not a maximum, and states are free to go above that. And, I mean, you really can't underestimate, like --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Again, for the carriers, that begs the question, but keep going. Yeah.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I mean, you know, the -- you know, the statistics that the amicus briefs have shown is that roughly 94 percent of carriers have not had, like, a safety exam by the federal government. And the way this works is, to get on the road in the first instance, you do not have to go through some -- I mean, it's -- it's kind of less rigorous than getting your state driver's license for a car. So --

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further?

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Would you take a minute and walk me through why you think or why it's -- it's agreed here that subsection (2) regulates safety in intrastate cases?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So -- oh, the argument would be that, you know, if you -- if -- if this were a (b) case, and I was trying to argue that safety is not -- is -- is not preempted, what I would do is I would make an argument sort of like I would make in the airline deregulation context, which is to say there were these tort claims beforehand, they probably -- like, the last thing anybody would have thought was going to be preempted beforehand was an intrastate tort claim.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: And all Congress was trying to do with (b) is to make sure when they deregulate interstate that there aren't these sort of confusing intrastate regulations of the same economic things. So that would basically be my argument. I -- you know, I could -- I could warm to it and try to get a little more textual and try to say that, you know, it's written a little bit differently, and it really does focus on intrastate rates, intrastate routes, intrastate services of any freight forwarder or broker. And it's just a little hard to think of these, like, negligent hiring things as being sort of, like, distinctly intrastate, but it's a harder case than (c), I'll -- I'll grant you that.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Was there ever any federal involvement during the ICC era with intrastate shipping?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: No, that was -- I mean, except in one or two areas where they felt like they had to do it to rationally regulate interstate, but the -- under the old ICC, the basic division of labor was interstate, federal; intrastate, state.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So why would this have any application at all just structurally to intrastate shipping? If -- if the deregulatory effort is to deregulate covered areas and this area was not intrastate, why would it have any application at all?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Oh, I -- I -- my -- I'm surmising now, but I think the idea was, okay, like, in a world where everything's regulated at the federal level, it's pretty easy to sort of hand off, like, regulation to the states when it's intrastate, but in a world where you really want the free markets to sort of prevail, having this little island of state economic regulation of intrastate starts to seem, like, less wholesome and it's sort of interfering with the federal objection -- objectives. And in this regard, it's probably worth recognizing, you know, the federal deregulation takes place in 1980. They don't impose the preemption provision generally until '94. And then they don't address brokers and freight forwarders until 1995. So, for 15 years, the federal government was perfectly happy to deregulate at the federal level and let the states kind of do what they want. And then, at a certain point, I'm surmising that they said some of the state regulation is getting in the way of our federal economic deregulatory objectives, but at the same time, you know, the one place -- well, in the two places because they did it for passenger too, they -- they really did try to preserve state regulatory authority with respect to safety in motor vehicles.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Alito? Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I -- I paused when I was reading the briefs in this case. Is the intrastate preemption subject to challenge? Because, if it's been decoupled from interstate commerce, under what congressional power could there -- could the government preempt?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I'm -- I'm guessing. I mean, it's probably a pretty good question for the federal government. If I had to make the argument for them, I would say that it's under the Necessary and Proper Clause, and it would be under the theory that they needed to regulate the intrastate in order to rationally deregulate the -- the interstate, but I -- I guess I'll take the question as a friendly amendment as just one more reason to leave the intrastate provision for a case where it's actually at issue because the one thing that's crystal-clear here is they have no (b) argument. This is interstate. And so --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yes. And I do understand what you're saying to Justice Kavanaugh and Alito, which is the difference between intrastate and interstate is even wider than whether you cover brokers or not because intrastate, they're preempting everything, not just whether it's in transport of property.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Right. And, you know, like, if I were going to warm to the topic of having to have a theory of how all this works, I suppose, if what they were really trying to do in 1995 is make sure that the state regulation sort of doesn't interfere, maybe they just address it a little bit differently.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Could I stop you to go back on the shipper question? Because one of my questions in my mind was how far back can you go. Could you go to the shipper? And you're saying in many states you can. Would that be for -- for hiring a negligent carrier or for hiring a negligent driver?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Meaning, is it auto -- is the liability automatic for the shipper?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So, under certain circumstances, and I think the most circumstances it would be for hiring a negligent carrier, but I think it also could get to the driver level. I mean, I think, you know, in a case like this, like, you know, and this happens more frequently than you think. I mean, I think one of the other statistics in the amicus brief is some 50 percent of all carriers only have one driver. So particularly if you're a broker, you may very well know not just what carrier you're getting but what driver you're getting.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Driver you're getting. But your point is that the shipper would not be preempted under any circumstance. So this lawsuit doesn't have a defense under this Act if you're suing the shipper?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: If you're suing the shipper, that's right. Now there is an amicus brief here that's, you know, or two actually, I think, on behalf of shippers that are making what I think is kind of an extravagant argument that they are covered by the preemption provision even though it obviously doesn't address shippers and it only addresses carriers, brokers, and freight forwarders.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you happen to know if there are lawsuits against shippers that go on routinely?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: My -- my understanding is that there are, and -- but my understanding is that, you know, it's also caused some shippers to start using brokers because --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Because it's a way to try to --

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- at least it gives you a colorable textual preemption argument to use a broker.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Got it. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Clement, the government has an argument -- I think it's the government, it might be the Respondents too -- that your theory makes this "with respect to motor vehicles" phrase superfluous. I was just wondering what you would say about that.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I was hoping you would ask. So I think there's a -- there's a big problem with that argument because I think it -- it sort of fails to appreciate how broad the definition of "transportation" is. And the statutory definition of "transportation" that's relevant for this part of the statute is reproduced at pages 18 and 19 of the petition if you have that. But it's incredibly broad definition. And it's -- it's 23(a). And the first thing in the definition is a motor vehicle, and then it then goes on to list 10 other things: Vessel, warehouse, wharf, pier, dock, yard, property, facility, instrumentality, or equipment of any kind. And then there's a (b) clause that goes on and talks about services related to movement, including receipt, delivery, elevation -- I had to look that one up and figured out they were actually talking about grain elevators -- transfer and transit, refrigeration, icing, ventilation, storage, handling, packaging, unpackaging, and interchange of passengers. So there is an enormous amount of things that come within the broad definition of "transportation" that do not involve motor vehicles. So there's no superfluity problem here at all. And I'd -- I would say that the breadth of that definition of "transportation" is maybe one more reason to think that maybe you should decide this case either just on the safety exception, or maybe you should say the preemption clause doesn't apply here at all to safety torts a la the lower court handling of the ADA cases because, if somebody gets injured on a wharf or a pier or a dock, there's no way that's with respect to a motor vehicle or it could certainly not be. And it would be pretty weird if that were preempted. But, of course, the old ICC had a pretty broad sense of what it got to regulate, including the routes and charges and services for, like, a grain elevator. So -- so I do think there are real reasons for caution here, but I'm glad you asked the question because I do think the more you appreciate the breadth of the statutory definition of "transportation," the less persuaded you would be by that superfluity argument.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: If you prevail here, I just want to pick up on what brokers will need to do. So you prevail here, then you're counsel to a broker, say, and the broker is worried about, you know, the issue The Wall Street Journal focused on yesterday, the English language proficiency of drivers who can't read signs, okay? And so the broker says: I don't want to select a carrier that hires drivers that are not proficient in English language. How do they do that? And do they need to do that? And how do they do that? Because that's a lot of the current discussion, is there are a lot of different issues, but that's one that's currently in the current events.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So a complicated question. I'd probably give them a pretty long memo, and, you know, I'd start by saying I'm not sure these, like, English language requirements are really sort of safety regulations with respect to motor vehicles. Maybe they are, I mean, but, like, I think that's a debatable question sort of at the threshold. And then --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, why not? If you can't read -- if you're hiring drivers who can't read the signs, that seems like a safety issue.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: If -- if -- if you say so. I mean, you know, I -- I -- like --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Assume that's true. Keep going then.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Okay. Assume that's true. Then I've got to, like -- you know, I want to address whether, like, I'm violating some other federal law by discriminating on the basis of English language proficiency. And I haven't sort of thought all that through, but it's not obvious to me that you can do that. But assuming you can --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, the federal government now tests for that for the drivers.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Yeah, I bet that's being challenged somewhere. But -- but -- but let's assume --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- that, you know, we're on to the last part of my memo, we'll assume that that's also legal, then I would say, okay, like, you can use large carriers, but if you do, make sure that they have an adequate testing program, and if you do, you're done. And -- and the carriers will have every incentive to have that themselves because everybody thinks the carriers are liable, so if we don't have problems with the two threshold questions, then the carriers are going to have to do it. So the brokers should be in a pretty good position. They just have to hire carriers that actually have a reasonable policy for dealing with that, and if they want to have smaller carriers, that's great, but they just need to --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The same for, let's say, drug use?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: They're asking the carriers what do you -- they've got to get something from the carriers that say we're checking our drivers for -- for drug use, alcohol background, safety checks, prior accidents, all that?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Right. But what you don't want them to do is not even ask that question --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- and then hire somebody who's cheaper and then -- because, you know, one of the reasons, I think, that you do want them to have to have some duty of care in these circumstances is this is a margin business.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So, if -- you know, if they don't have the -- any sort of incentive to internalize any of the costs of not asking the question, they really have no good reason to ask the question. They want to hire the cheapest carrier.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So the real-world effect, again -- and I think this may help your position, so this is not a hostile question -- is the -- the brokers are going to be really digging into the carriers' safety background checks of drivers if -- if you prevail in this case, and that, I don't think you should shy away from that. I just want to make sure that's what you think is going to happen.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Yeah, but, you know, I think it's -- it -- I -- I worry a little bit about digging into because -- because, you know, in fairness --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, they want to -- they don't want to get sued for millions of dollars, so they should dig in, shouldn't they, if you're their counsel?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I'd tell them -- as their counsel, I'd probably say, out of an abundance of caution, dig in, but I would also say I wouldn't -- you know, I wouldn't, like, be alarmist about this because the carriers -- everybody agrees the carriers are going to have to do this.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: And so, as long as you're not hiring fly-by-night carriers who aren't doing it and are -- have the minimum $750,000 of insurance, like -- and you can hire small, you can hire -- you know, small is beautiful if -- if they do it right. But you do have to sort of ask those questions and make sure that, you know, they're in a position where they are doing everything they can. And then, if you do that, I would think the broker is not going to have a problem if it's asking the hard questions of the carrier.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Clement, assume that I -- and this is just an assumption -- assume that I think (c)(1) doesn't apply and it's not preempted. What would be the advantage of deciding this case based on the safety exception rather than the preemption provision?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: So the principal advantage of deciding this case on (c)(2), on the -- (c)(2)(B) or whatever it is, like, that might not really move you if you actually don't think it's preempted under the principal preemption provision, but the principal --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: I said that backwards. I mean say that I think you might win under (c)(1) because I don't think it's preempted.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Why would I decide it, or is there any reason to decide it?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Under those circumstances, there's not much. But I will say this. Like, you know, I think the question about whether the ADA preemption provision applies to safety-related torts is a hard one.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: And I think anything you say about the principal preemption provision here is, I think, going to have some carryover effects in the ADA context. That's, after all, why the Airlines for America filed an amicus brief urging you to sort of decide it that way. And I think you might want to hold that issue, which is a tricky one, harmless. And the way you hold that issue completely harmless is save it for another day when it actually matters, and you just decide this case on the basis of text that's not in the ADA.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Let me flip it. Let me say that I am very -- I do think it's a hard question. I'm very skeptical about whether the preemption provision applies or not. Is there anything that -- and let's say that I think that you win under the safety exception. Is there anything that an opinion might say about the safety exception that might prejudice that question when we confront it later, the preemption provision?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I don't really see how it could because it's text that's not in the ADA. And I suppose everything I'm saying about the ADA would also be if you ever do get one of these (b) cases and -- you know, with intrastate, that would be another thing that you're not prejudging if you decide this case just based on the text of the safety exception.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Same on (c)(1) because the text is the same in the ADA?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Yeah. Exactly. Exactly.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So I guess I'm still struggling to see how the policy concerns that you explored with Justice Kavanaugh really are being addressed in this statute. I mean, this is not a liability statute as I see it. It's not setting up a cause of action for any kind of trucking accident. It's leaving it to the state. So the concerns about the extent to which the brokers are involved in this or will -- you know, other kinds of defendants or how much liability, don't those come out state by state depending upon what claims are being made as you read this statute?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: No, I mean, that's absolutely true. And, you know, you have an amicus brief from 28 states and they pulled off the incredible feat of having California and Texas on the same amicus brief.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: And, you know, that shows there is a lot of variety in the state tort laws. And I think -- you know, there are a couple of states that don't have this tort. But it does go all the way back to the First Restatement, and most states, the overwhelming majority of states, have this tort. But, like --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And if -- and the ones that don't, then the brokers in that state wouldn't be held liable for them, or all the arguments about, like, how -- how do we do this seem like they'd be made to the state legislature who's trying to hold them liable. It's not in this statute, I think, because it doesn't suggest to me, looking at this text, that Congress was focused on the result of this, other than to say we are preempting with respect to rates, et cetera, et cetera. Can I just ask you one more thing? The -- the government is focused a lot on the definition of "motor carriers" that is in this statute, and I guess I'm wondering whether that really matters from the standpoint of how we understand your arguments related to (c). Is this turning at all on us focused on the definition? I thought this was about, in your view, with related -- what is it -- "with respect to" as opposed to the definition of "motor vehicles."

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Right. There's a -- there's a couple of things there, I mean --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: -- which is to say, you know, that there's some focus on motor -- the definition of "motor carrier" or "motor vehicle." But, like, I don't think any of that matters here for the reason that you said, both that we're really thinking about, like, the state judgment, whether it's a state judgment about safety, and then, at the same time, like, nobody doubts there's a motor vehicle here.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So it doesn't matter -- we -- we don't have to focus in resolving this case on the definition of "motor vehicle" in your view?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: I -- I don't think so. Like, nobody really disputes that there's, like, a motor vehicle at the center of this case that caused, you know, grave injuries to my client. So the only question is, is sort of the state safety regulation sufficiently closely related to that motor vehicle? And as I've tried to explain, it really is quite closely tethered because the whole reason you have a negligent hiring tort is because you're talking about something that poses a danger of harm to third parties, and the thing that poses the danger of harm to third parties in this context isn't some brokerage forum. It's the 80,000-pound truck.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Boutrous.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Congress assigned freight brokers a defined role to arrange for transportation of property by connecting shippers with motor carriers. Petitioner's tort claims target that core service, so the claims are preempted. The only real question here is whether these claims are saved as the safety regulatory authority of a state with respect to motor vehicles. They are not. Brokers lack sufficient connection to motor vehicles. Brokers don't own, operate, or control motor vehicles. Text, context, history, and precedent confirm this. A few examples. First, the savings clause uses the phrase "with respect to motor vehicles," which, in this Court's words in Dan's City, is massively limiting, and it applies specifically only to motor vehicles -- the definitions do matter here -- not transportation of property, which is much broader by statutory definition. Second, subsection (b) expressly preempts state law claims related to intrastate broker services and has no savings clause. It is unthinkable, it's absurd that Congress would have saved states' authority to regulate interstate but not within its own borders. Third, Congress required only carriers to insure against personal injury liability for motor vehicle accidents and only required brokers to have insurance for freight charges. And that mirrored what the ICC said just a few years earlier, that brokers were not responsible for personal injury damages. And it was the expert agency. Finally, this isn't a deeply rooted tort. We challenged Petitioner to find one single negligent selection case involving a federally licensed broker in American history and at common law, and they came up empty. It didn't start until 2004. Petitioner's asking this Court to disrupt the uniform federal regime by green-lighting new state law duties even more onerous than the special checking system that the Court said was preempted in Rowe v. New Hampshire Motor. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: If brokers are as attenuated in the process as you say, wouldn't they fall out in a tort action from a causation standpoint, proximate cause standpoint?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Only after a great deal of litigation, Your Honor. What happens in these cases is the brokers are now named automatically. And if you look at the complaint, it's all information and belief. This -- this notion that the company violated its own policies regarding a conditional rating, information and belief. They -- they do this in every case. It's basically strict liability claims against the brokers for having connected the shipper and the carrier. And so, after a lot of litigation, the burdens are tremendous. C.H. Robinson is a big company, has many, many of these cases pending, when it did nothing wrong and when the drivers and the carriers did nothing wrong. So it burdens interstate commerce. And Mr. Clement was arguing for a patchwork, the very patchwork that Congress intended to end, of states imposing their varied standards.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: But you -- the -- you mentioned that you can't find cases from an earlier time. Isn't that a part that brokers are playing a more pronounced role now than they did in the past?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Well, my client's been around for over a hundred years. Brokers have been involved all along in the process. The cases -- I love the L.B. Foster case that Mr. Clement banks his whole case on. It was a 1969 case involving a shipper -- the broker was not even at issue on appeal -- who had hired an unlicensed broker, so there was a broker involved, and an unlicensed carrier. That's the best they could do. There wasn't a federally licensed broker or federally licensed carrier involved. His Restatement example, Your Honor, he -- he -- he wants to -- us to ignore what the ICC said in 1987, that brokers are not responsible for personal injury liability, and assume Congress knew that in 1934 the Restatement cited a case about a hotel concierge whose omnibus was broken, so he hired someone else to take people to the train depot. That's the history he's relying on. The reason there isn't a history on this, Justice Thomas, is that the -- the ICC and the federal regime was viewed by the states and by the -- by this Court in Castle as being the -- the -- the -- the entity that says whether carriers could traverse in interstate commerce. So there were no -- states could not deem a carrier or a driver unfit. That would thwart the federal licensing regime that existed then and that exists now.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: What was the treatment by the ICC of intrastate shipping, purely intrastate? And there were certain products that were not covered by the ICC. How were those treated?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: I -- I think what the ICC would have probably -- they would have captured those in its regulation to the extent they were within the chain of interstate commerce. And this goes to some of the questions about Section (b). I -- I do think the quote from Mr. Clement that he has no answer, no idea why Congress would have had preemption intrastate be complete, with no saving clause, but to have saved these claims interstate. But I think that to the extent in the chain of interstate commerce -- and this goes to Justice Sotomayor's question about constitutionality -- I think there is a connection between purely intrastate activities and interstate commerce in some instances. The other point I wanted to -- to make is the -- the briefs and Mr. Clement today really hammer on this notion that Congress was looking at economic deregulation, and safety, of course, it -- it was not touching. It clearly cared about safety. The -- the FMCSA, the fed -- the expert agency that now exists, has enormous federal power over safety and wrote 700 pages of regulations, and it's in a partnership with the states who endorse -- I think most, if not all, have endorsed the safety standards of the federal government.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Yes, it -- it cared about it in -- in the way that you're talking about, but, really, the -- the relevant focus, I think, needs to be on what it was doing in this statute. And what concerns me about the way you've set this up is you are suggesting, I think, that the preemption part of this was also covering state regulations with respect to brokers as they relate to safety and that then, when Congress came in and put in the -- the -- the -- the savings clause as a part of this, it was leaving brokers preempted with respect to what they do that might implicate safety. But that's not the way I think the statute reads. It seems like the preemption is smaller than you're giving it credit for to begin with.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: The -- the preemption is very broad. As, again, Mr. Clement kindly outlined, transportation of property is -- that's what's preempted.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No, no, no.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Laws related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Or broker with respect to transportation of property.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Transportation of property. But -- but -- but I think what's doing a lot of the work in this, in understanding the scope of preemption, is the states may not enact or enforce a law related to a price, route, or service. And if you read it that way, then the savings clause is focused on what the state can do concerning safety.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: No. Well, it is, Your Honor, but it's safety with respect to motor vehicles. And the -- the preemption clause, everyone -- we're not disputing that a tort claim that -- that targets the services of the broker -- and this goes to the essence, as the Seventh Circuit said, as Aspen said in the Eleventh Circuit, this is the essence. What brokers do is match the carrier. Counsel's depiction of what brokers do is completely divorced from reality. They're not interviewing and investigating. They're matching up federally approved carriers with shippers, and they want them to be safe because they want their shipper clients to keep hiring.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: There's no duty of the broker to -- to -- to check the safety records when it's doing that match?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: No, Your Honor. In fact, they can rely -- they're supposed to rely on the federal government's licensing. That's what the court said in -- in --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: As a matter of state law, the state --

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- could not impose a duty on them to make sure that they pick and match the -- the -- the best in terms of safety record?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: That -- that's exactly the question in this case, Your Honor. And -- and if you look at the complaint, JA 23 through -- 22 through 26, they're asking this Court to -- to green-light the states to impose duty after duty.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: What was the status quo? Was the state able to hold a broker liable before this action, before the preemption, for things like what is happening in this case?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: No, Your Honor, we don't believe they were. We think this Court's Castle decision talks about how the states could not second-guess the federal authorization of a carrier because it would thwart uniformity, it would thwart commerce. This isn't a traditional area of fed -- of state prerogative. This is about interstate commerce --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, Mr. --

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: -- and the free flow of interstate commerce.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Boutrous, this might be a good idea, this might be a bad idea. I mean, maybe this is just a simple-minded way of looking at this, but I'm just looking at this provision, and it says "shall not restrict the safety regulatory authority of a state with respect to motor vehicles." And you agreed that these tort actions are part of the state's safety regulatory authority. So then the question is whether these suits are with respect to motor vehicles. And, you know, come on, how could they not be? They're all about getting good drivers behind the wheel of a massive truck. That's with respect to motor vehicles.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: That -- that, Your Honor, I think, is actually a good way to look at it because, first, the definitions matter here. It -- the Congress, when it added brokers and added section -- subsection (b), which has no saving clause, it didn't change the -- the saving clause, the safety saving clause, it didn't -- it broadened it to transportation of property. It kept it focused on motor vehicles. Motor vehicles are very narrowly described as the tractor trailer, the truck.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. But the motor vehicle is the truck. And these suits are all about the trucks --

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: And -- and as this --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- and who's driving the trucks.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: And -- and I go back to -- and, again, Petitioner did not mention this two -- this Court's two leading cases. The Dan's City case, this Court said with respect to transportation of property, which, as Mr. Clement said, is really broad, that that massively limited the scope of preemption.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Could the state say you can't drive a truck unless you're 16? Unless you're over 21?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: I think the states could have.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Oh, so that's not with respect to the motor vehicle. It's with respect to the driver who's going to drive it. Can the state say truck drivers have to wear seatbelts?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: That would be compatible with federal law, Your Honor, and they could do that.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. So what's incompatible with federal law with respect to safety to say you shouldn't operate this if you're a careless, negligent driver?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: The states can say that. What they can't say is that a broker can be held liable.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Why not? Because the broker is the one putting the driver in the seat --

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: That's not correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- of the motor vehicle that's causing the accident.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: That's not correct, Your Honor. That's another real, I would say, not accurate depiction in the briefing here. The --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Wait a minute.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The broker hires the company who's going to transport, says the company is negligent because it's been -- it's had, I think, two or three accidents in the space of a month, the drivers have, and it only has nine trucks, and it has a driver who just that month had another crash where he was found negligent. I don't see how someone hasn't failed in their duty.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that would be the carrier and -- and the driver. The broker doesn't know all that. That --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, then that's a defense, counsel.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Yeah, it is a defense. And it's also why there needs to be --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But it's not -- it -- it doesn't answer the substantive question of whether it could be held liable if it had a way to check.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: It -- if it was required to check the special checking system, and I go back to Rowe, where this Court said that the main statute there that would require the carriers to check to see if the packages were going to be directed to a minor shipping tobacco, that that would have imposed this patchwork and would have imposed this extra duty on the services of the carriers. And so --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Safety regulations always create a patchwork. No matter what the safety issue is, there's going to be different state requirements, and so the patchwork exists as -- just because of the exception.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: But can I address that, Your Honor? Because Section 31 -- 31141, which both parties cite, seeks to eliminate that. It gives the Secretary pretty broad power to preempt after decision. So, while the states can enact their own safety regulations, they must be compatible with federal law, consistent with federal law even if they're stricter, and if they burden interstate commerce because they create a patchwork, because -- and we're talking about driving through multiple states. Here, it was Ohio, Texas, and Arkansas, I think. And so you need to have certainty that you can traverse those jurisdictions lawfully.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: With the -- on the "with respect to motor vehicles" phrase again, going back to that, obviously, motor vehicles are involved. I thought your argument was you have to figure out how broadly to read "with respect to," like, "relating to" --

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- it could go forever, or where do you cut it off. And I guess my question is, given the safety implications, why would Congress have wanted to cut it off at carriers and not include brokers when, I think, although they haven't traditionally done it, brokers could do more to ensure that carriers are hiring safe drivers who don't have issues with their history or whether they can read the signs or whatever it might be, and that will reduce the number of fatal trucking accidents, which is a huge problem? Why is that not a reasonable way to read it?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: It -- it's -- I think that Congress -- what Congress did was ensure that brokers could allow for a seamless interstate commerce. If it imposed those obligations, it would -- it would just completely bollix up the broker duty. They would have to investigate whether the driver was fit. This is just right from the complaint. The carrier was -- they'd have to investigate the driver history that -- and ask for that. And so it --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, how -- how will that happen? Will they demand -- I mean, they'll demand from the carriers, give us information about who you're hiring. I assume that's what will happen if you do not prevail in this case.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Right. We have to demand that.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That can be done, though, right?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: It could be done, but these things happen quickly. This is about matching carriers who are on the road with ship -- shippers' demands to get things there just in time, and -- and it's become a crucial part of interstate commerce. And I do want to go back, Your Honor, if I can, on the interpretation of the language. We do believe that "with respect to motor vehicles" is the crucial question here. And one thing we haven't heard --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Mr. Boutrous?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: I'm -- I'm sorry to interrupt you there, but -- so you -- you -- you agree, with respect to motor vehicles and safety regulations, it accounts for the safety belt regulation, it accounts for age limits and things like that. You agree that it also permits a hiring tort suit against the carrier. Do you agree that it also permits a tort suit against the shipper?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: We do not agree with that, Your Honor.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. So it stops with -- with the carrier and only the carrier?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Exactly, Your Honor. And -- and that's not to say there isn't rigorous federal enforcement. There are criminal provisions.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Why not the shipper?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: The shipper, by imposing that duty on the shipper -- and there are lots of claims against shippers. The shipper, by imposing that duty -- this is like Rowe. If you impose that duty on the shipper, then they're going to make different choices with respect to motor carriers. They're not going to allow new entrants. That will have -- that will have an impact on the services of the motor carriers. And that's sort of how the Court analyzed it in Rowe. So I think it would burden them tremendously, and it would basically --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: It's really a causation argument. You're saying it's just too far removed.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: It's --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: It's a proximate causation argument in effect.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: It's -- it's -- it's related to it, but I think it proves our point. We all know proximate --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: If it's -- if it's a causation argument, I guess I'm back to where Justice Thomas is. I understand the burden of -- of these lawsuits. I appreciate that. But it -- if your -- to the extent your argument hinges on causation, that is built into the state tort system in these -- in these cases. I suppose, if there weren't a proximate cause element that's required, that might be a matter of some consideration, but there is.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: The problem with that is it is baked into the state system. They want to be, in the words of the Ohio brief, laboratories of democracy. They're going to have varied standards. They're going to create a patchwork, which would cause brokers and shippers and -- and --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, right now, we have a proximate cause standard before us, right?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: In -- in this --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: In this case?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Well, that's because Petitioners are asking the Court -- Petitioner's asking this Court to assume Congress was thinking about proximate cause. It was thinking about --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, I'm asking about the claim in this case. There's a proximate cause?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Yes, there -- there is. And -- but the problem is it's a nebulous concept. And I go back to the definitions in "with respect to motor vehicles." Motor vehicle safety is defined as the performance of a motor vehicle in a way that protects the public interest against unreasonable risk of accidents occurring because of the design, construction, or performance of a motor vehicle. So, when Congress used "motor vehicle" in "with respect to motor vehicle," it was, in this Court's words, massively limiting the scope of the saving clause. And, here, the brokers are too far removed. They can't -- they don't touch the vehicle. They don't control it. They don't know what vehicle is going to be used. They don't know who -- who the driver is. But they are performing a function relying on the federal authorization and licensing, is -- which is what -- what Congress in -- intended. And so I think it's real important -- and on -- on the federalism point, I think the Nebraska brief points out that under this regime, the state with the most plaintiff-generous tort principles and proximate cause principles, they'll rule the United States. Everyone will have to adhere to those standards or avoid that state entirely. And that's why Congress felt that preemption was important here.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: How difficult is it for a carrier to be authorized under federal law?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: It's not nearly as easy as Petitioner's suggesting. Again, I think the regulations and the statutes speak for themselves. They have to show that they're fit. They have to show that they meet the -- the various standards, that they will agree to hold drivers to certain standards. They do audit carriers. A lot of these complaints, we disagree with them about the FMCSA, but if that -- that's a complaint, well, then they could do more or go back to Congress. And going back to the insurance point, yes, some carriers don't have significant enough insurance.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, do the -- do the federal authorities insist that someone who's seeking authorization show that they've done background checks on drivers, that drivers can read signs, that drivers don't take drugs, don't have a history of alcohol abuse, any of the things that would contribute to a real safety threat?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: They're -- they're required to do that and -- and -- and -- and -- and accomplish those goals to get federal licensure. And if they don't do it, the FMCSA can take -- take away their license. So there's a rigorous system.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas?

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: I think I alluded to this before, but has there been an increase in the role of brokers post-deregulation?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: There has been, Your Honor, and it's contributed greatly to our economy and to commerce and the free flow of commerce.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: How significant?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Significant, Your Honor. So it was --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: No, I mean how significant has the increased role been?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: I think back -- back before deregulation, there were more -- you know, less than a hundred brokers, and now there's thousands. Most of them are much smaller than C.H. Robinson, which is another reason not to impose these brand-new duties.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Alito, anything further? Justice Gorsuch -- Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: There's, amici says, somewhere between 6- to 9,000 motor carriers registered with the FMCSA every month. You're going to tell me that they have the resources to check every one of those new entrants and all the existing ones to see whether they're following the rules?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: They -- they require that the carriers swear they will conform.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: They -- I am asking you, do you believe the agency has anywhere near the resources necessary to adequately vet all of those applicants and keep on top of the operation of the people who already are licensed?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: It's a significant task, Your Honor, and -- and probably not. They couldn't do it with respect to every one.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: But --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So Congress did one thing. It exempted motor vehicle safety. You still haven't given me a reason in this context why, with such a capacious word like "with respect to," that we should read it narrowly instead.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Your Honor, in the spectrum, "related" is the gold standard for breadth in preemption. "With respect to" is used next to "related" in Section (c)(1). So Congress clearly was thinking of it differently. This Court, in cases like Presley and Lamar, which the Eleventh Circuit cited and the -- the Chamber of Commerce brief cites, this Court said "with respect to" means direct relationship to or impact on. And, here, to hold someone liable for negligence with respect to a motor vehicle, where the -- they had no ability to control it or inspect it, and those duties that Your Honor -- or the -- the big group of carriers, now that would be imposed on these brokers, who aren't safety experts, who would -- they'd have to -- we'd have to hire an army to do what --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: -- they're asking the Court to green-light.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Two things. You said it started in 2004. Why?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Because the creative plaintiffs' bar decided to start bringing these cases against brokers and to establish bringing them into the case to have additional targets. That's what's really ironic here. The notion that Congress, when it -- when it preempted claims against brokers --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Because some of the carriers couldn't pay or --

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: I think they -- some of them couldn't pay or they -- they wanted to have additional potential defendants. And so they're not asking this Court to restore or protect longstanding state law. They're asking this Court to authorize the states to become laboratories of democracy, to creatively adopt a patchwork of new duties that will -- will really hamper interstate commerce and -- and defy Congress's intent.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And then you've had, I think, law against you in the Ninth Circuit since 2020, so -- and you've talked about, oh, this would be difficult to impossible. What have brokers been doing in the Ninth Circuit since 2020? Because I assume they have adjusted their behavior in response to that and --

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: They've been --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- the Sixth Circuit as well.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Yes. They've been litigating because they're getting sued still repeatedly. There has --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But are they changing their practices at the front end so they're not liable at the back end given the Ninth Circuit and Sixth Circuit law and the possibility that that law will -- will stay the law?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: They -- they have not made the radical change, waiting for a ruling from this Court to clarify that these claims cannot be recognized.
