But, in California, the effect has been mitigated a bit -- a bit because two state courts of appeal have rejected the Miller decision and gone with Judge -- Judge Fernandez's dissent in that case, which I think really just very in simple terms outlined that this is a step too far, I think the Eleventh Circuit said a bridge too far, to hold someone who -- liable for -- who matched the carrier in the -- the --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I'm puzzled that no one's -- I mean, I'm not saying you know everything that's going on out there, but I'm puzzled that brokers haven't started doing more on the theory that they could be held liable. Wouldn't they change their behavior with some bad law?

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Well, the problem is they -- they get you coming and going because, if you do more, then the -- the argument is like, in this case, you're vicariously liable because you had control. So, if you don't do enough --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: -- they say you should have inspected like you were the federal agency. If you do too much or you do anything, they say you're in control, which is just not true. The broker is a broker. They're a middle person matchmaking so commerce can flow.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Jackson? Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: BOUTROUS
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Joshi. SUPPORTING THE RESPONDENTS

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I think, if you look at the phrase "with respect to motor vehicles" in isolation, Petitioner has a pretty good point about how to interpret just those words in isolation. But I don't think it works in this statute. And I really focus on the contrast between paragraphs 1 and 2. Paragraph 1 uses the phrase "with respect to the transportation of property," paragraph 2 "with respect to motor vehicles." That seems like a conscious choice that Congress made to parallel the language but change the noun to a much narrower noun. As Mr. Clement observed, "transportation" has, like, 24 or 25 different nouns I think I counted, one of which is motor vehicles. So that suggests that "with respect to motor vehicles" must not include things that are with respect to the other stuff in the definition of "transportation," one of which is arranging for the movement of property, which is what a broker does. Put it this way. Suppose there were a rule that applied with respect to coffee, cream, and sugar and then an exception that applied only with respect to coffee. You would naturally think that the exception does not apply with respect to cream and sugar, even though that phrase in isolation might cause you to adopt the opposite view. I think that's kind of what's going on here, and that's why I think Respondents' reading of the statute is the better one. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Is there any daylight between your argument and that of the Respondent?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I don't think so. I -- I think we're aligned on the interpretation of the safety exception.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: With respect to the use or the role of brokers, how significantly has that increased?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I agree with what Mr. Boutrous said. It has increased markedly. Before the deregulation, there were only several hundred brokers, is my understanding. Today, there are approximately 25,000 registered -- federally registered brokers operating, from small sizes to as large as C.H. Robinson.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: What role did the ICC play in regulating transportation in intrastate before deregulation?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I -- I don't know exactly the extent of the role. My understanding is that they generally would have thought that -- that intrastate regulation has a substantial effect on interstate commerce, and so, to the extent there would be inconsistent rules, as -- as both Mr. Clement and Mr. Boutrous mentioned, the Castle case is, you know, from the 1960s, I think, and -- and that really exemplifies these things. Illinois there, you know, had certain regulations for trucks, but this Court said that Illinois cannot use a violation of those in order to revoke the operating authority even for the intrastate trucks.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Counsel, both the counsel for Respondent and for Petitioner were asked a fair number of questions about (b)(1). And I don't want to evaluate it, but I think they were basically each saying they don't quite know why it's there. And I wonder if the federal government has an answer.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I think my answer is that Congress did not view the safety exception as reaching these kinds of claims against brokers and freight forwarders. And so I don't think there is an anomaly. And there certainly isn't if you adopt Respondents' view of the -- of the safety exception. Now what Mr. Clement said, first, he said he has no idea, but that's just how it is. He said, regardless of this case, I think he said there's going to be this difference between intrastate and interstate anyway. But I don't think that's correct. The first thing he pointed to was that (b) does not include the limiting phrase "with respect to the transportation of property" the way (c)(1) does. But you don't need that phrase in (b) because the only entities covered in (b) are freight forwarders and brokers, and the only thing those guys do is act as middlemen for the transportation of property. That's what they're statutorily defined as being. So you don't need that limitation in (b). You do need it in (c) because it applies to motor carriers, which can have their cars on the road not engaged in interstate transportation of property. So I don't think that works. So I -- I really do think there's a huge anomaly here that Congress in '95, where, you know, we all acknowledge broker -- claims against brokers were not preempted before 1995, and then Congress decides in '95 we need to add brokers. And the way they do it is they, as Mr. Clement said, insert them in (c) and add this provision (b). And it just makes no sense whatsoever that a Congress interested in preempting claims against brokers would leave greater state authority over interstate broker services than intrastate. That is so weird. I am unaware of any preemption regime -- express, implied, field, conflict, obstacle, whatever -- in which that's how it works.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Mr. --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: It is so bizarre that I think you really should look at the safety exception the way Respondents do because that's the only way to avoid --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But let me ask you a question, Mr. Joshi, because I think we could also believe that Congress was really interested in making sure that safety was top of mind, priority. And the problem, I think, with the argument in the way that you've set it up is that you are assuming away any responsibility that a broker might have for safety. Your argument depends in a way on us accepting what you've said, what Mr. Boutrous said, that brokers are just operating as middlemen, they really don't have anything to do with safety. And I guess I don't understand why we have to believe that Congress shared that view as opposed to Congress thinking: Well, to the extent that brokers are involved in safety, that's going to come out in the tort suit as a causation issue. And so we don't really have to take a position on which individual defendants are actually responsible for safety. What we want to do is make sure that safety regulations are preserved, that safety is top of mind for everyone, and if brokers really are the kinds of middlemen that Mr. Boutrous and you say, they're ultimately not going to be held liable because either the state's not going to allow this kind of tort suit on the front end or they're not going to be held liable because they're really not a big player in this from the standpoint of tort law. Why -- why can't -- that to me is perfectly consistent with the view that Petitioners have and make your view seem a little odd.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: So two responses to that. Well, first, I agree, what you just said is consistent with what Mr. Clement says, but, obviously, I disagree with it. So the -- the two stat -- a couple of statutory points that I think show that Congress, in fact, was thinking that brokers wouldn't be responsible for safety, so they did share the view that Mr. Boutrous and I are advancing today. Number one, on the safety exception, it would have been the easiest thing in the world for Congress to just repeat "with respect to transportation," just like it had half a sentence earlier, or just leave it out entirely.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But wouldn't -- wouldn't the easier thing to be to -- and say something about brokers in the safety exception? I mean, if you're right and they shared this view that there were certain individuals in the chain who were just not going to fit, then I would think we would see that language in the -- in the safety exception, right?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Sure. Congress could always be more clear, but what I'm saying is that Congress's choice there suggests that they did not necessarily view it this way. But there's more. There's much more. Congress has an entire chapter, several chapters, of the U.S. Code in Title 49 that deal with safety addressing carriers, safety of motor vehicles, driver qualifications, and they're all addressed at carriers. Not a single one is addressed at brokers. And I'm going to now invoke, you know, they say the exception sometimes proves the rule. In 2012, Congress passed a statute and they added a provision and that provision makes transportation intermediaries, which I think we all agree includes brokers and freight forwarders, liable, and it says, you know, you should not coerce a driver or a carrier to violate all these numerous safety rules that apply only to carriers and drivers.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: As a matter of federal law?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: As a matter of federal law.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Right. Isn't that --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: And so it's a little bit odd --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No, but -- but -- but what I'm saying is this statute expressly preserves the scope of state safety regulations. So it's no answer, I think, to point out that Congress, under federal law, would not have held these brokers liable.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Well -- well --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: The question is, what were they doing when they said here we are preserving states' ability --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Right. No. No. I -- I -- I took your original question to be why do we think Congress shared the view that brokers would not be responsible for safety. And what I'm saying is the entire federal scheme is set up so that carriers and drivers who, to use tort law terms, are the lowest-cost-avoider in terms of safety are responsible for safety and they have to pledge that they're responsible for safety and obey all the safety rules as a condition of federal registration. And then brokers select from the pool of registered carriers. And --

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, that -- that makes -- that makes perfect sense if, in fact, there is -- the -- the federal authorization does require some -- does involve some inquiry into the safety of carriers. And do you want to say something about that, how it works as a practical matter?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: As a practical matter, you're -- you're right. And Justice Sotomayor was right earlier. You know, FMCA is -- is understaffed, overworked, can't possibly review the 700,000 federally registered carriers. But every year -- or I should say this. Of the 700,000 federally registered carriers, the agency has been able to do at least one roadside inspection for -- for two-thirds of those carriers in the last five years. So there is some oversight, not nearly enough. I will -- I will take -- I will grant the point. But carriers who violate their requirements under the statute or the regulations, not only can the agency revoke their operating authority and go after them, there is a federal private cause of action against a carrier who violates the federal requirements that an injured party can bring against a carrier for violating it. In fact, there's one against brokers too. Now, of course, I'm -- I'm not going to tell you it's frequently invoked because, obviously, the federal requirements for brokers don't involve safety, so I don't think there's a lot of federal cause -- you know, federal lawsuits based on it. But Congress did provide these as a means to ensure that carriers and brokers obey federal law.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas? Justice Alito?

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: (c)(2) wouldn't come into play unless (c)(1) applies. Would -- could you just say something briefly about why (c)(1) applies?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yes. Because a broker is defined as someone who -- you know, I'm looking at 13102(2), the back end of the definition, arranges -- arranging for transportation by a motor carrier. That is literally the service of a broker. And Petitioner's tort claim, if successful, would allow enforcement of a common law rule, and the common law rule would say not just that the driver was negligent on this particular occasion, and it wouldn't just say that C.H. Robinson erred in selecting Caribe on that occasion to transport those particular flower pots in December of 2017. Instead, it would say, Caribe, you should not -- you should not have hired -- or, C.H. Robinson, you should not have hired Caribe at all, and, in fact, no broker should ever have hired Caribe to do anything. So it's directly attacking a broker's selection of a carrier, which is the core service, which means it's related to the service of a broker. And that is why every court of appeals, even ones that agree with Petitioner, have rejected this claim and have agreed that these sorts of torts fall within (c)(1).

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I think you told Justice Thomas that you agree with everything that Mr. Montgomery is arguing. Is that correct?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: No, we're on the opposite side from Mr. Montgomery.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry, not Mr. Montgomery. What Mr. Caribe's counsel would say.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah, we're -- we're -- we're supporting Respondents' view.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're agreeing that the safety -- they -- they've argued, but I didn't see it in your brief, that the safety exception preserves only states' authority at the time of the Act's enactment?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: No, no. That's one where we say --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. And so I also didn't think that you were agreeing with his implied preemption argument, or are you?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: We're -- we're -- we don't take a position. We're saying you don't need to.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So it's not everything?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I agree with everything Mr. Boutrous said at the lectern today --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. So, right now, you would have it that the federal government is not imposing any safety obligations on brokers, and you're saying, despite the exemption for safety with -- with respect to motor vehicles, neither can states, so nobody's checking what they're doing?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Brokers are required to pick from among the pool of federally registered carriers. That's what does the work here. And --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So, even if someone has had a thousand accidents and it's well known, no -- no broker's ever liable?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: The -- the carriers would be liable for that, but, correct.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Thank you.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: The broker -- as long as they still are --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I just want to know the --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- the end result of your argument. No one's checking?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: That -- that is --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And no one's going to be liable?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: That is -- that is -- well, carriers are liable. But that is what we think the federal system is -- how it's supposed to work. Carriers are responsible for safety. It should be registered --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: How about shippers? Are you agreeing with your adversary --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: We --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- on shippers?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: We have not taken a position on shippers. I don't think -- I mean, just standing here at the lectern, it doesn't seem like this statute covers claims against shippers except in so --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yeah, it's hard to do.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: -- except insofar as through the Rowe against New Hampshire Motor Transport Association. There, that was a rule that actually regulated shippers, but it was nonetheless preempted because it affected carrier services. So, under that way, perhaps it could, you know, preempt certain types of rules against shippers, but it's sort of hard to see. There may be other --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Hard to see that a safety one would?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I don't know the answer to that --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Thank you.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: -- frankly.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I take it when you stood up, you said, okay, if you look at "with respect to motor vehicles," that seems pretty bad for you. But we're going to look further. And the place where you pointed was the definition of "transportation." Is that right? And I'm doing this on the fly, so forgive me, you can just tell me if I'm wrong, but you said the key thing about the definition of "transportation" was that it specifically says that transportation includes services related to arranging for, blah, blah, blah, you know, the transport of property. Is that -- and you said that's what a broker does. So we should think about that as in the other "with respect to" clause, not in the "with respect to motor vehicles" clause. Do I have that right?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. So -- but that provision also, you know, in addition to saying services related to arranging for transport, it says services related to receipt, delivery, transfer in transit. You know, it has all these other verbs in it too which it seems to me would apply to the motor carriers themselves. So, if we want to apply that same theory of the case, we would have to take out the motor carriers from the "with respect to motor vehicles" phrase.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Maybe, maybe not. I mean, motor carriers are the ones who own and operate the vehicles. And so, to the extent what you're challenging is they're performing those services, I -- I think maybe not. But more to the point, I think most of these claims against drivers are for negligence, and those are obviously permitted. They're not preempted. And federal law, you know, by operation of federal law and the definition of "employee," drivers of commercial motor vehicles are deemed to be employees of motor carriers even if they're independent contractors. That's express in -- in the definition. And everyone understands the reason for that is so that motor carriers will always be vicariously liable for the torts of their drivers during the -- the scope of employment. That's why you don't see in this complaint, which is in the Joint Appendix, you don't see a claim against the motor carrier here for negligently selecting the driver. That would be silly. No -- no sane plaintiff would bring that claim because you can just say the driver was negligent on this occasion, and therefore, you are vicariously liable. And that's typically why these claims against motor carriers aren't going to be preempted.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I mean, I take that point. All I'm suggesting is that you pointed us to a phrase as being --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- like, very relevant to you when that phrase includes just about everybody. It's not like a phrase about brokers where you say, oh, look, you know, they meant brokers to be in this provision because this is a phrase about brokers. This is a definition that includes everybody under the sun.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: That is true, and --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So I guess I'm -- I'm seeing --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- I'm not seeing how relevant it is --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: So --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- to the question of whether "with respect to motor vehicles" somehow excludes brokers.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: So I want to be clear. I -- I -- I think this particular point I'm making, as well as the three other main contextual clues that we've made in the brief, one of which is the intrastate, the other is the superfluity, potential superfluity, and the third being the financial services requirements, which Justice Kavanaugh was discussing earlier, I'm not making a claim that any of those four itself is dispositive and just answers the question. I do think this is a hard case, but I think all four of them tilt in Respondents' direction, and they suggest that "with respect to motor vehicles" has a narrower effect than it might seem. Dan's City supports that reading of "with respect to" as well. And given that every contextual clue, every oddity, every anomaly, Respondents' reading doesn't produce it, whereas Petitioner's does, I think, in the end, when you look at all of it, Respondents' is the better reading. That --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: -- that's our point.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch?

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: I just want to follow up on that a little bit. I -- I take your point that "with respect to transportation" is a lot broader than "with respect to motor vehicles." It's a good point. But, once you say that "with respect to motor vehicles" doesn't just mean with respect to the mechanics of that vehicle but can include hiring, negligent hiring, by the carrier or some negligence by the carrier, then I -- I -- I wonder why that doesn't also hold for brokers.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah. So -- so, to be clear, the definition of "motor vehicle" is not just tractor trailer, et cetera.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: But it also says it has to be "and used on a highway in transportation."

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: So claims about the use count --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: -- under the safety exception.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: So -- and that's my point, is once that camel's nose is under the tent and you can have a claim for negligent hiring against the carrier, why not against the broker?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: No, that -- that's my point. As far as I'm aware, there's no such thing as a negligent hiring claim against the carrier because the carrier is vicariously liable for the driver, so it would be -- so to -- a negligent hiring --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Or negligence by the driver. Once -- once -- once you've gotten to the driver --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- okay, and not just the motor vehicle itself, and a negligence in operation or use of the motor vehicle counts, then --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Well, "use" counts because it's in the definition.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: I understand that. I -- I -- I -- I'm not arguing with you about that.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Once you concede that the use counts and that somebody can be held liable for negligence in the use, then it just seems to me we're talking about causation at that point.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I take the point. And, obviously, that is Mr. Clement's point here today, but my -- my point is that --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: And I under -- and I understand as well -- I'm sorry to interrupt -- but, you know, I'll let you answer. I understand as well, if there were a strict liability regime or something like that, but so long as we have a proximate cause regime, and that's all that's before us, all right, now go ahead and answer.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah. I mean, I guess -- I guess the way I look at it is transportation is really broad. And, yes, it says motor vehicle is one of them. And it's got other stuff that may be closer to motor vehicles, may be farther away. I think, you know, 20 -- 13102(23)(a) says motor vehicle, vessel, warehouse, et cetera, and then, in (b), it's services related to the movement, including arranging for. And I know how you could see that "arranging for the transport of property" is kind of related to motor vehicles, but given Congress's choice to use "with respect to transportation" in 1, in paragraph 1, and "with respect to motor vehicles" in 2, that suggests it wants a subset, and the question is, what is in that subset and what is out of the subset?

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: I agree with that.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: And I think arranging for transportation is --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: But, once you've gotten to use of the motor vehicle --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah, but --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: -- is into, right, you say that's --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Right, yeah.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: You -- you -- you -- you -- you spot them that. I'm not arguing with you about that. I -- you -- you -- you've given that up, fine. But now the use of the motor vehicle, not just the motor vehicle itself but the use of the motor vehicle can be regulated by the state.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yes, but -- but brokers don't use vehicles, right? Brokers don't control the vehicles. The broker doesn't know how -- when the brakes --

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah, but if somebody was --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: -- were last inspected, how thick the tire tread is. Brokers don't even know the safety record of the driver. That's confidential.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: Right. But you can have vicarious liability.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: No.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: You -- you spot that.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: No, for carriers, not for brokers.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: No, I understand. But you spot vicarious liability for carriers. I'm not arguing with you.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Under federal law, it's express.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: I -- I -- I'll stop.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I -- I mean, I take the point that this could be a -- you know, what you described could be a sensible system, but I think, given the federal statutes and regulations, I don't think it's what we have today.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: A couple things. You at the beginning said "related to" and "with respect to" are different in your view.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Probably.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And that's not very convincing to me.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah, agreed.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So I just --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah. Look --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- want that get that out there. I mean, I think, with either term, you have to look at the context and common sense to see, as Judge Fernandez said in his dissent in the Ninth Circuit, where to draw the line, Palsgraf, et cetera.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I -- yeah, I -- I actually agree completely. I mean, I think --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: -- this Court in -- in Lamar and Archer made the point that --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That -- that -- I'm going to move on to my next question --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- if you agree completely.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So you say the carriers are liable, so don't worry about it, but one of the problems, I think, that's led to this is the carriers sometimes can't pay.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So, when we're thinking about context and common sense in figuring out how broadly to read "with respect to," isn't that part of the context and common sense?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Well, it's the statutory context.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: So we have to look at it from Congress's perspective. And from Congress's perspective, carriers could pay because they were -- they are required to have the financial security requirements of 750,000, and, yes, that was enacted in 1980. I take the point. Maybe it's woefully inadequate.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: But -- but --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Two more quick ones. You just said brokers don't know the safety records of the drivers.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Aren't they going to have to, if the other side prevails in this case, figure out how to -- how to get into that?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I -- I mean, those are confidential. The carriers --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: By -- by virtue of what?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: By privacy laws and federal law when -- when FMCSA reviews it. So -- so, right now, I don't think brokers have a practical way to get it. And what that means is that brokers will, as some of the amicus briefs point out and as I think Mr. Clement just candidly admitted, acknowledged, that brokers are going to follow the, you know, no one ever got fired for buying IBM principal and they're just going to go to the largest carriers. It's going to be FedEx, UPS, J.B. Hunt, Penske, you know, et cetera, and --

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That leads to my last question, which is, again, context and common sense, and their position's going to mean fewer truck accidents.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: There are going to be fewer trucks. There are going to be fewer carriers in the market.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Fewer truck accidents where people get killed. And they say because brokers are going to dig in and not hire or arrange with shaky truck drivers.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: That -- that could well be true, but that would be a result of there being fewer trucks on the road, which means worse interstate commercial motor delivery services. I mean, if Petitioner's tort claim is successful, it's going to take the pool of registered carriers that federal law says brokers can pick between, and it's going to shrink the pool. That is contrary to Congress's deregulatory purposes.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I just want to give you a chance to explain as quickly as you can what your surplusage argument is because the way you've defined this, you have motor vehicles as a subset, not as the whole thing, in terms of the preemption. So why there would be surplusage here?

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: There would be a surplusage on Petitioner's reading of the statute. Petitioner seems to suggest that "with respect to" just means concern, and it can be a pretty lengthy chain, pretty indirect. I mean, I think he -- Mr. Clement today at the lectern said indirect. He wants it to be interpreted the same as "related to" in Morales and, you know, the ADA cases, which includes indirect connections. If that's true, then every single thing, as I think Justice Kagan was pointing out, in the definition of "transportation" arguably is indirectly related to a motor vehicle, which means that the safety exception --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Although he says --

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: -- you could blue pencil --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I'm sorry, he says -- he says -- he says with transportation a property is like grain elevators, it has nothing to do with the car or with the motor vehicle. He's got transportation of property much broader.

turns.name: JOSHI
turns.text: I don't know how you get the grain into the elevator or out of the elevator without a motor vehicle. So I would think that on Petitioner's logic, that -- claims about that would also be in. So I think, if you take Petitioner's interpretation seriously, then the -- the safety exception basically reads the same if you blue pencil the phrase "with respect to motor vehicles" out of the statute.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Mr. Clement?

turns.name: CLEMENT
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. Just a few points in rebuttal. First, a couple of times my friends on the other side have crept into this alternative theory that really this should be -- "motor vehicles" should be limited to owners and operators. Obviously, those words aren't in the relevant statutory provisions. And as we point out in the reply brief, as a practical matter, something like 45 percent of carriers don't actually own the -- the vehicles. They lease them or they belong to the driver. If we're going to talk practicalities, I think one really important number that's practical is that 94 percent of the registered carriers out there on the roads haven't had any meaningful federal safety inspection, the kind of annual inspection you have. You get the registration, and then, hopefully, within about a year or so, you get the fuller inspection. But 94 percent of the carriers out there haven't gotten this federal inspection. So it would be really nice if state tort law provided a backstop to a federal system that's not really doing a lot. On the (b) point, my -- my friend from the Solicitor General's office said, you know, the freight forwarders are just -- are -- are just middlemen. That's not actually true. And that's why there is this requirement that they have some personal injury liability insurance. They do do more than the brokers, but the point is brokers aren't just middlemen at least in many of these cases. I talked about the cases where the carrier is actually captive. All they do is take C.H. Robinson loads. That's all they do. They don't serve any other broker or they don't serve shippers directly. In that situation, C.H. Robinson knows or should know every bit as much as a carrier. The issue of the -- oh, and one other point on this, just the practicalities of this. There are services out there right now that tell brokers more safety information that's available based on the federal records. C.H. Robinson doesn't want to use those, but other brokers do. Maybe that's why C.H. Robinson is a defendant in more of these cases than some of the other brokers. There's more that brokers can do. There was some discussion of a patchwork. That really doesn't work here because they're -- they concede there's going to be a patchwork with respect to the carriers. And this whole regime on safety allows for a patchwork. It's a -- the -- the federal safety regulations are expressly designed as a minimum, and states are allowed to go above and beyond the minimum. All right. There was some discussion of this L.B. Foster case, and my friend said, well, that's not such a great case for Mr. Clement because that was an unregistered broker and unregistered carrier. But the problem is their theory sweeps so broadly that you can't hold a broker liable for negligently hiring a completely unregistered carrier. There are some cases like that. And that just doesn't make any sense whatsoever. That's complete negligence, and there's no reason there would be immunity there. There was some brief discussion of the Castle case. That old chestnut, if you take a look at it, it basically says Illinois can fine drivers for violating their state law. They just can't literally bar them from the state. But, you know, this is a situation much more analogous to the fines, that there would be liability. It's going to mean, if it's a single company driver, like, it might put the carrier out of business, this particular carrier here has dissolved itself as an Indiana corporation, but I think that's probably a good thing, not a bad thing. Some discussion just about how to resolve this case. I would say, if you are convinced that we win on (c)(2), then I would urge you not to say anything really definitive about (c)(1). My friend is correct that all the courts that have looked at this particular issue, with the safety exception staring them in the face, have all said that (c)(1) provides preemption. But the hard case is going to be in the ADA context. The hard case is going to be, if there ever is a (b) case -- there probably won't because they're so few that are purely intrastate -- but the hard case is going to be when you're staring at that preemption clause and there is no safety exception to sort of back it up. So I would urge you, if you think (c)(2) applies here, just leave (c)(1) for another day. And then I'm sure I missed some of the subtlety of the Solicitor General's officer point about the coffee and the cream and the sugar and all of that. But the thing is, if you have one of these, like, McDonald's torts where the problem is the piping-hot coffee spilled in your lap, I would say that's a tort with respect to coffee. And for the same reasons, this is a tort with respect to motor vehicles. This case doesn't have to be that hard. The thing that triggers state tort liability is an 80,000-pound motor vehicle. That's what devastatingly injured my client. This is a case with respect to motor vehicles. We urge you to reverse.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
